claim: The Interactions API provides server-side state management, eliminating the need to manually manage conversation history on the client side by using a previous_interaction_id parameter
actionable: When building agents, use the previous_interaction_id returned from each API call to attach to existing conversation history instead of maintaining a client-side history array
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: Implicit prompt caching with server-side state is 2-3x more effective than client-side state management because the server preserves exact context, preventing cache hits from breaking due to formatting changes like whitespace or line breaks
actionable: Rely on server-side state management in the Interactions API rather than client-side history to maximize cache hit rates and reduce input token costs
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: Gemini Flash (3.0) works effectively as a coding agent model when provided with good instructions, skills, and context—it doesn't require the largest model for agentic tasks if the instructions are clear
actionable: Use Gemini Flash rather than larger models for coding agent tasks when you have well-defined skills, system prompts, and context
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: Skills (MCP tools) should include high-level information and links to living documentation rather than exhaustive API documentation to reduce sync friction and knowledge cutoff issues
actionable: When creating agent skills, embed links to up-to-date markdown documentation instead of hard-coding all API details into the skill definition
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: Gemini 3.1 Flash Live is a native audio model that processes sound tokens directly rather than cascading transcription → LLM → text-to-speech, resulting in lower latency and better multilingual support
actionable: Use Gemini Live for real-time conversational applications where native audio processing provides latency and multilingual advantages over cascading pipelines
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: The Live API supports audio-only sessions up to 15 minutes and audio-video sessions up to 2 minutes before termination, but context window compression can extend this by using a sliding window approach
actionable: When building live sessions, enable context window compression if you need sessions longer than 15 minutes (audio-only) or 2 minutes (audio-video)
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: Ephemeral tokens generated server-side can be sent to clients for direct WebSocket connection to the Live API, reducing latency compared to a proxy-based server-to-server approach
actionable: Generate short-lived ephemeral tokens on your backend and pass them to clients to enable direct client-to-API WebSocket connections with lower latency
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor DeepMind

claim: Gemini Live can understand mixed-language speech (e.g., Denglish) naturally and switch between languages in the same utterance due to native audio modeling
actionable: Build multilingual voice agents with Gemini Live for users who naturally mix languages, without special handling
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: System instructions and guardrails in the Live API are the primary mechanism to prevent hallucinations and off-topic behavior; structured system prompts are more effective than relying on model capabilities alone
actionable: Invest in well-structured system instructions with clear guidelines, tool definitions, and guardrails rather than expecting the model to infer constraints
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: Tool combination in the Interactions API (e.g., combining Google Search with custom functions) was a frequently requested feature that is now supported
actionable: Use tool combination in the Interactions API to mix built-in tools like Google Search with your custom function calls
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: For real-time conversational grounding on code or specialized context, Gemini Flash (non-real-time) may be preferable to Live API because it provides better observability and control at each pipeline step
actionable: Evaluate whether you need fully real-time interaction; if not, use Gemini Flash with image input and web fetch for better observability and context grounding
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: The Live API currently lacks built-in speaker identification; while it can remember speaker names from conversation, it cannot filter audio input by speaker identity to prevent unauthorized commands
actionable: Do not rely on speaker identification for security-critical voice agent tasks; implement additional authentication or context-based filtering to prevent unauthorized commands
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: The Interactions API does not automatically store audio transcripts; applications must implement custom storage or rely on partner integrations (LifeKit, PipeCast, etc.) for transcript and audit logging
actionable: Plan for transcript storage early in voice agent design; evaluate partner integrations if compliance or audit logging is required
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: The Live API supports thinking levels (none, low, high) that trade off latency for reasoning depth; higher thinking increases latency
actionable: Set thinking level to 'none' for fast response times or 'high' when reasoning quality is more important than latency
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: Server-side state in Interactions API is free on the free tier (1 day retention) but stored for 55 days on paid tier; retention can be customized further on Vertex
actionable: Plan conversation architecture around retention limits: 1 day free tier, 55 days paid API, flexible on Vertex
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: Recursion is the idiomatic pattern for tool use in agents: check for function calls, execute them, append results, and call the agent method again until no tool calls remain
actionable: Implement agent loops using recursion: after checking interaction.outputs for function calls, execute the function, create tool results, and recursively call the agent method
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: JSON schema definitions are required for tool use so the model knows what parameters to generate; the schema must exactly match the Python implementation
actionable: For each tool, create matching JSON schema (for the model) and Python implementation (for execution), ensuring parameter names and types align
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: Conversation branching is possible by storing interaction IDs on the client and using interactions.get() to retrieve full context history, allowing parallel exploration from any historical point
actionable: Store all interaction IDs from your conversation; use interactions.get() to retrieve context and branch from any historical point for parallel reasoning
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: Lyra 3 is a music generation model that can generate both 30-second clips and full songs, supporting lyrics and genre specification in conversational agents
actionable: Use Lyra 3 as a tool in conversational agents to generate music on demand based on user requests for genre, theme, and style
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind

claim: For real-time conversational context grounding in coding, sending a single image with audio input is more efficient than streaming continuous image frames because one image is approximately 1200 tokens
actionable: In live coding agents, send a single screenshot + audio input rather than continuous video frames to preserve context window while still providing visual grounding
source_title: Building Conversational Agents — Thor Schaeff and Philipp Schmid, Google DeepMind
